text: The surgeon uses robotic hook cautery to dissect the gastrohepatic ligament, with a robotic arm temporarily retracting the liver for improved access to the hiatus. The gastrohepatic ligament is fully dissected, revealing the gastroesophageal membrane. The right crus is visible to the left, highlighting important anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to dissect anteriorly, exposing the esophagus. The short gastric vessels are taken down, further mobilizing the stomach, with the spleen visible in the background.
The surgeon completes further mobilization of the esophagus, focusing on the left crux of the diaphragm, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visualizes the gastric diverticulum after complete mobilization of the stomach and esophagus. Further dissection is performed to delineate the borders of the diverticulum.
A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction to aid in manipulation. The surgeon continues to mobilize the esophagus further into the mediastinum.

Using upper endoscopy, the surgeon confirms the presence and base of the gastric diverticulum. The gastric diverticulectomy is completed with two fires of the robotic sixty millimeter stapler, followed by endoscopic confirmation of an intact staple line and successful resection of the diverticulum base.

The surgeon closes a small hiatal hernia using an O non-absorbable barb suture. Following the closure, the shoeshine maneuver is performed to facilitate the creation of the Nissen fundoplication, although this step is not visually shown. The wrap is completed by suturing the anterior gastric wall to the anterior surface of the esophagus for approximation.
The surgeon passes the final U stitch to complete the wrap around the esophagus and stomach, securing the surgical repair.
